conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility properly, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you with your query.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children under the age of 16, or children aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 17 years old. He's not in school at the moment, but he's recently started training to become an electrician - it's an apprenticeship programme.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information about Alex. 

To help me assess your eligibility, I need to understand a bit more about Alex's apprenticeship. Could you please tell me: **Where is Alex doing his apprenticeship - is it in England, or elsewhere in the UK (Scotland, Wales, or Northern Ireland)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's doing it here in England - we live in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help me assess your eligibility.

**Do you live with Alex, or does he live elsewhere?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. Now I need to ask: **Are you currently living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I am. Like I said, we're in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you. One final question: **Is Alex currently in paid work, and if so, how many hours per week does he work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not working at the moment - just focusing on his apprenticeship training.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official government guidance, I need to inform you of the following:

According to the rules, you'll normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in **approved education or training**. However, there's a specific rule that states: **you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts an apprenticeship in England**.

Unfortunately, since Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England, this means you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for him, even though he's under 20 and in training.

Let me record this outcome for you.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex. 

The reason is that while Child Benefit normally continues for young people under 20 who are in approved education or training, there is a specific rule that stops Child Benefit when a child starts an apprenticeship in England. Since Alex has recently started his electrician apprenticeship in England, this disqualifies him from Child Benefit eligibility.

If you have any questions about this decision or your circumstances change, you can contact the Child Benefit Office for further guidance.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period; Child has started an apprenticeship in England
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, while Child Benefit normally continues for children under 20 in approved education or training, there is a specific exclusion: Child Benefit stops if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Since Alex has started an English apprenticeship, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him, despite him being under 20 and living at home with the claimant.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex. While the claimant meets the general residency requirements and is responsible for Alex who lives with them, Alex has recently started an apprenticeship in England. Under the specific Child Benefit rules, starting an apprenticeship in England is a disqualifying factor that stops Child Benefit entitlement, even for children under 20. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and has started an apprenticeship in England. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, while Child Benefit normally continues for children under 20 in approved education or training, there is a specific exclusion: Child Benefit stops if your child starts an apprenticeship in England. Since Alex has started an English apprenticeship, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him, despite him being under 20 and living at home with the claimant.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex. While the claimant meets the general residency requirements and is responsible for Alex who lives with them, Alex has recently started an apprenticeship in England. Under the specific Child Benefit rules, starting an apprenticeship in England is a disqualifying factor that stops Child Benefit entitlement, even for children under 20. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
